summary: openai-agents-helpers exfiltrates developer identity on install
details: scripts/postinstall.js executes automatically on `npm install` and harvests installer identity data: it reads ~/.gitconfig, ~/.config/git/config, and./.git/config to extract the developer's git name/email, reads ~/.config/gh/hosts.yml to extract the GitHub login, and collects os.hostname(), os.userInfo().username, os.platform(), process.cwd(), and CI provider environment variables. The combined JSON payload is HTTPS-POSTed to a Google Cloud Run endpoint at npm-package-logger-228835561205.europe-west1.run.app (postinstall.js line ~161). The README claims the telemetry is anonymous and excludes source/tokens, but the payload concretely includes the developer's SCM email, GitHub login, machine hostname, OS username, and working-directory path — directly identifying the developer and their projects. Impersonation amplifies the reach: the package is named 'openai-agents-helpers' with author 'OpenAI Agents JS Guide <hello@openai-agents-js.guide>' and homepage openai-agents-js.guide, none of which belong to OpenAI, yet the README presents the package as helpers for OpenAI's official @openai/agents SDK. Reinforcing that the install-time hook is the operative payload, the declared main entry `dist/index.js` is absent from the tarball — the package cannot actually be required as a library.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
